title: US Energy Policy: Ceding — not seeding — the terrain
summary: Elizabeth Sherwood-Randall, Fall 2017 Fisher Family Fellow at the Future of Diplomacy Project and Fellow at the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs outlined the U.S. Department of Energy's role in diplomacy, "energy diplomacy," while she was Deputy Secretary for the Department. The conversation was moderated by Nicholas Burns, Faculty Director of the Future of Diplomacy Project and Roy and Barbara Goodman Family Professor of the Practice of Diplomacy and International Relations.
body: “Energy is an instrument of our diplomacy,” Dr. Elizabeth Sherwood-Randall, Fall 2017 Fisher Family Fellow noted at the opening her conversation with Faculty Director, Nicholas Burns on November 27, 2017. In the 1980s, the US pursued diplomatic means to get and keep access to oil from Arab Gulf states. In the 1990s, diplomacy was instrumental in bolstering the newly independent Central Asian states for energy resources, and to decrease dependence on the Gulf. “Today,” she described, “it’s very different — it is more sophisticated and 'multidimensional'.” Under Sherwood-Randall’s leadership as Deputy Secretary for the U.S. Department of Energy - a post she left January 2017 - the United States had pursued three specific energy diplomacy goals that leveraged America's unique capacity for innovation in science and technology: Open and make more efficient energy markets so that resource dependence cannot be used as a weapon Increase resource development to reduce or capture carbon emissions Ensure effective management of civil nuclear technology around the world. The energy diplomats in her department did much more than just “back-room deals” in pursuit of these goals, she said. They ran energy dialogues bilaterally and trilaterally and worked not only with the UN Security Council's Permanent members but with other subgroupings. They created a new Clean Energy Ministerial in 2009 to support knowledge exchanges across governments on clean energy technologies. With the White House, they launched the Nuclear Security Summit in 2010 aimed at preventing nuclear terrorism globally. In addition, her colleagues had developed a clean energy research center for U.S. and China researchers to work together on carbon capture projects among others. Perhaps the most demonstrable of the critical role that energy diplomats could play was as technical experts at on the Iran nuclear deal negotiations. That experience, she noted, had been exactly as chronicled in Harvard Kennedy School Professor Meghan O’Sullivan’s new book Windfall: How New Energy Abundance Upends Global Politics and Strengthens America's Power . “She wrote about what I lived.” These initiatives and international exchanges were possible because of “constant tending, constant gardening” that built relationships between the U.S. Department of Energy and its international counterparts. Also, energy was seen as and used as a tool of the U.S. government in Sherwood-Randall’s tenure to be “organized, orchestrated, and prioritized alongside other objectives of the U.S. government.” The U.S. opening of diplomatic relations with Cuba, for example, highlighted future energy opportunities for Cuba. In contrast, Sherwood-Randall described the present U.S. administration's prioritization of international political objectives as the U.S. abdicating critical components of its international role. She warned: “It is likely that countries that do not share [U.S] values will step in to fill the gap [left by American disengagement internationally]. We are ceding this terrain, not seeding it as we should be. I believe practitioners and experts like those at the Kennedy School need to speak out.” The latest from Belfer See more US Energy Policy: Ceding — not seeding — the terrain Print Asia & the Pacific Diplomacy Energy Europe Future of Diplomacy Project Nuclear Issues Russia Related Elizabeth D. Sherwood-Randall More From This Expert At the Brink: Loose Nukes The Urgent Need for a National Biosecurity Initiative Related Nicholas Burns More From This Expert NATO is Vital to U.S. National Security Nicholas Burns on His Time as U.S. Ambassador to China and on Efforts to Address Climate Change Up Next Presentations Move 37 Presentation at the Global AI Impact Summit, February 20, 2026 Feb. 20, 2026 From Emerging Technology, Scientific Advancement, and Global Policy Photo Credit Toggle MSC Event Summary Rupture: (Re)thinking Middle Powers in a Fragmented World Feb. 14, 2026 The Problem of China in the British Foreign Office Mind by Oliver Yule-Smith December 2025

_list.source: Future of Diplomacy Project, Belfer Center

result.main_content_en: The article discusses the evolution of U.S. energy diplomacy, contrasting historical approaches with the more sophisticated, multidimensional strategy pursued under Deputy Secretary of Energy Elizabeth Sherwood-Randall until 2017. This strategy focused on three core goals: opening and making energy markets more efficient to prevent resource weaponization, increasing resource development to reduce carbon emissions, and ensuring effective global management of civil nuclear technology. Implementation involved bilateral and trilateral energy dialogues, the creation of the Clean Energy Ministerial, the launch of the Nuclear Security Summit, and the establishment of a U.S.-China clean energy research center. Energy diplomats played critical technical roles, notably in the Iran nuclear deal negotiations. The article emphasizes that this proactive, relationship-building approach required constant effort and integration with broader U.S. government objectives. It concludes with a warning from Sherwood-Randall that the current U.S. administration's disengagement represents an abdication of international leadership, ceding strategic terrain to competitors who do not share American values.
result.main_content_zh: 本文探讨了美国能源外交的演变，对比了历史做法与伊丽莎白·舍伍德-兰德尔在2017年离任能源部副部长前所推行的更复杂、多维的战略。该战略聚焦三个核心目标：开放并提高能源市场效率以防止资源被武器化；增加资源开发以减少碳排放；确保全球民用核技术的有效管理。实施手段包括双边和三边能源对话、创立清洁能源部长会议、发起核安全峰会以及建立美中清洁能源联合研究中心。能源外交官扮演了关键的技术专家角色，特别是在伊朗核协议谈判中。文章强调，这种积极主动、注重关系建设的方法需要持续努力，并与美国政府更广泛的目标相协调。文章最后引述舍伍德-兰德尔的警告，指出当前美国政府在国际事务上的退出，意味着放弃了其国际领导角色的关键部分，正在将战略阵地让给那些与美国价值观不同的竞争对手，而非进行应有的培育。
